output: LoRA stands for Low-Rank Adaptation. Instead of updating every weight in a large language model during fine-tuning, LoRA learns a small pair of low-rank matrices for each target layer. The base weights stay frozen, so the trainable parameter count drops by orders of magnitude while the model still adapts to the new task.

Perplexity is the exponential of the average per-token negative log-likelihood. Lower is better: a perplexity of 1 means the model predicts the next token perfectly, while higher values mean the model is more uncertain. Perplexity is the standard intrinsic metric for language models because it directly measures how well the predicted distribution matches the true text.

instruction: Translate the following English sentence into French.
input: The cat sits on the mat.
output: Le chat est assis sur le tapis.

instruction: Write a one-line summary of attention.
output: Attention lets each output position focus on a learned, weighted mixture of all input positions, so the model can pull in the parts of the context that matter most for each prediction.

instruction: Why do we use cross-entropy loss for language modeling?
output: Cross-entropy loss measures the negative log-likelihood the model assigns to the correct next token. Minimizing it pushes the predicted distribution toward the empirical distribution of the training data, which is exactly what we want when training a model to predict text.
